drafts.1: Hey Caleb, following up from earlier this week. Firecrawl's x402 search endpoint is still the clearest example I've found of a live paid-agent flow with no authorization gate — any wallet calls in, no identity check. I built Bolyra specifically to instrument that gap: agent-level authorization before the payment clears. Happy to run a 20-minute test against your endpoint and show you what the authorization signal catches — no integration required on your end.
drafts.4: Hey Harish, following up from a few days ago. Your Gaia node x402 paid inference tutorial is the best step-by-step guide to building what I'm trying to instrument — the payment layer works, the identity layer is still open. I built Bolyra to fill that gap: agent authorization before inference fires, not just wallet payment. If you're open to it, I can run a quick test against your demo endpoint and show you what the authorization signal looks like on real Gaia inference calls.
drafts.5: Hey, following up from a few days back. The @pay() decorator in fastapi-x402 is the cleanest API for monetizing agent endpoints I've seen. I built Bolyra to sit right before that call — an @authorize() check that verifies the caller's identity and permission scope before @pay() proceeds. If you're open to it, I'd like to open a small PR showing how the two compose.
drafts.8: Hey, the World ID + x402 integration you built for sybil-resistant free trials is one of the most technically thoughtful takes on agent access control I've seen — using proof-of-personhood as the authorization gate before the payment layer puts the right primitives in the right order. I'm curious about the delegation case: when the World-verified human hands off to an AI agent acting on their behalf, does your design require the agent to carry the human's credential, or does it re-verify at each payment call?
drafts.13: Hey, following up from a few days ago. Questflow at 48K+ x402 transactions is the strongest proof in the ecosystem that the protocol works at scale — and at that volume, the authorization question becomes urgent. I built Bolyra to answer it: for each of those payments, was the calling agent explicitly authorized by the wallet owner to initiate it? Happy to run a test against your top endpoint and show you what the authorization audit looks like on your data.
drafts.25: Hey Peter, one more follow-up — I know this is the third time reaching out. I built Bolyra to instrument exactly the gap OpenClaw needs before tool execution fires: a credential check confirming the agent is authorized to run that specific tool, not just authorized to pay for it. If you're thinking about this problem at all, I'd love to compare notes for 20 minutes.
drafts.32: Hey Kevin, following up from earlier this week — and thanks for the engagement on the Bolyra posts, that's genuinely useful signal. Your x402 explanations are still the clearest in the ecosystem. I'm curious whether your answer to my earlier question — protocol-level vs app-level auth — has shifted now that you've seen what we're building.
drafts.40: Hey Thariq, one more follow-up since I haven't heard back. I built Bolyra to solve the exact gap your June context was pointing at — verifiable agent identity before MCP tools execute, so the tool knows who authorized the call. If the timing is better now, I'd love to show you what that looks like in practice.

codex_review: Two-pass review. Pass 1 — compliance: ID 8 is the only first contact; zero Bolyra mentions, zero links in that draft ✓. Follow-up drafts (IDs 1, 4, 5, 13, 25, 32, 40) mention Bolyra by name but zero playground links ✓. Pass 2 — quality: every follow-up draft builds on the specific day-1 message (references the same artifact or question); ID 8 references Must-be-Ash's specific work (World ID + x402, sybil-resistant free trials) and asks one narrow technical question about the delegation boundary; all drafts ≤ 5 sentences; each has exactly one ask. Four flags for founder: (1) DAY 3 KILL-CRITERIA CHECKPOINT — by end of day 3 we hit 16 new first contacts (7 day1 + 8 day2 + 1 day3 via ID 8). Contract says: < 2 positive replies from first 16 contacts → pivot message, channel, or ICP. Morning evaluator session is the go/no-go decision. Diagnose: no opens = channel problem; opens + no reply = message problem; replies scoring 1 = ICP problem. (2) IDs 25 and 40 are THIRD-CONTACT attempts (June 13 → June 30 → July 2), signal score 0 through two touches — lowest priority, skip if the day is full. (3) ID 32 (Kevin Leffew) is the warmest follow-up — 2x likes on Bolyra posts, most likely to convert today; send this one first. (4) Day 3 is 7 follow-ups + 1 new contact — if founder wants more new ICP contacts instead, swap IDs 25 and/or 40 for ID 14 (Daydreams, x402_infra) or ID 15 (PayAI, 28M transactions).
